critique.summary: The article discusses a 2015 study by two geographers in Connecticut who found that the presence of solar panels on nearby houses was the strongest predictor of whether a homeowner would install solar panels themselves. This '1 kilometer effect' was later confirmed in studies from Sweden, China, and Germany, showing that proximity influences adoption of solar technology and reflecting a broader social phenomenon where people tend to adopt behaviors similar to those around them.
mainPoints: Early adopters of solar panels are influenced by interest in technology, trusted installers, and perceived benefits.
The biggest predictor for solar panel adoption is having neighbors within one kilometer who already have solar panels.
This proximity effect has been replicated in multiple countries including Sweden, China, and Germany.
The phenomenon illustrates a broader social principle where people tend to adopt behaviors prevalent in their social or physical proximity.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes findings from studies on solar panel adoption that highlight the importance of social and spatial proximity in influencing behavior. The '1 kilometer effect' is supported by research cited in TED Ideas and replicated internationally, lending credibility to the claims. However, the article does not provide direct citations or detailed data from the original studies, which limits verification. The extension of the principle to general social behavior is a reasonable interpretation but more anecdotal and less rigorously supported within this article.
considerations: The article does not specify the names or publications of the original geographers or studies, making it difficult to verify the primary sources.
Other factors influencing solar panel adoption, such as government incentives, economic status, or environmental attitudes, are not discussed.
The generalization of the proximity principle to broader social behaviors is presented without empirical evidence in this article.
The article relies on a secondary source (TED Ideas) rather than primary research publications.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the '1 Kilometer Effect' in solar panel adoption, exploring social and economic factors influencing neighbors' decisions. Commenters reference theories like Diffusion of Innovations and Social Proof, while also considering alternative explanations such as door-to-door sales and local subsidies.

comments.author: jasonhong
comments.text:
Two concepts that help explain the original article are Diffusion of Innovations and Social Proof.

Diffusion of Innovations is a widely cited theory explaining why people do or don't adopt any kind of innovation, from boiling water to eating limes on British ships to installing telephones. The concept of innovators, early adopters, and late adopters comes from this theory. More relevant to this post is that this theory posits five factors contributing to adoption, one of which is Observability: you can easily see other people gaining benefit from an innovation. The more Observable an innovation, the more likely it is to be adopted. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diffusion_of_innovations

The other is Social Proof. Seeing what other people are doing, especially those that are similar to you in some way, can help steer your behavior, often in subtle and unconscious ways. There are studies about how simple signs like "people who stayed in this hotel room re-used their towels" or "most of your neighbors are reducing their electricity usage too" can shift people's behaviors, even without people explicitly realizing it. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_proof

My colleagues and I used these concepts in several pieces of research on what we called Social Cybersecurity (joking that the term "Social Security" was already taken). The insight we had was that cybersecurity has very low observability, making it hard for innovations to diffuse through one's social network. That is, I don't know what your cybersecurity practices are, and vice versa, making it hard for best practices to be adopted.

One intervention we did was a large-scale intervention on Facebook to improve observability, showing that simple messages like "108 of your friends use extra security settings" did increase clickthru and adoption rates of those settings. https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2660267.2660271

We also have many other studies along similar lines, e.g. many triggers for talking about and adopting security are social in nature (https://www.usenix.org/system/files/conference/soups2014/sou...), that security settings that are more social in nature are more likely to be adopted (https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2675133.2675225), and more.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-supported theoretical framework with citations and relevant research, making it the most accurate and insightful contribution.

comments.author: xg15
comments.text:
Kind of reminds me of "nucleation sites" in physics.

I think one factor that's missing from the explanation is the extensive media and political coverage that solar panels got: There are probably very few people by now that don't know what a roof solar panel is or who don't have an opinion on them.

So my guess is that most of those neighbors who "suddenly" decided to also get a panel, were already interested or at least curious about getting one. (In the sense of "I should totally be getting one some time, but I have no time/now idea how to start/other things are more important/etc")

Maybe the early adopter was then what changed peoples' stance from a vague idea to a concrete plan.
comments.analysis: Offers a plausible complementary explanation emphasizing prior awareness and interest, adding depth to the social contagion theory.

comments.author: heisenbit
comments.text: Hmm, this actually could have policy implications. If one wants to subsidize a new technology and allocated a limited budget often the first movers are getting it and those first movers may be clusters which is not ideal driving broad adoption. So creating very small geo buckets for adoption oriented subsidies would make sense.
comments.analysis: Insightful policy suggestion grounded in the observed clustering effect, though it assumes subsidy effectiveness without direct evidence.

comments.author: dragontamer
comments.text:
How about an alternative explanation?

What if a substantial amount of local solar contractors are doing door to door sales? Or other locality/proximity based sales (signs, driving a car with ads on it, and the like?)
comments.analysis: Presents a reasonable alternative explanation that could explain local clustering, though it lacks direct evidence.

comments.author: jandrese
comments.text: Some of it may also be neighborhoods where solar contractors went door to door selling the systems.  Even if you don't buy from that salesman you get the numbers in your head and start to realize it isn't some exotic tech for elite weirdos.
comments.analysis: Supports the door-to-door sales hypothesis with a practical perspective, reinforcing the possible role of sales tactics.

comments.author: orev
comments.text:
For solar panels, many people might be interested but also concerned how they look. When another neighbor gets them, people will get used to how they look, realize it’s not so bad, then be more likely to get them.

I don’t think the final conclusion necessarily follows, not with this example. Solar panels are big and obvious on top of the house. It’s not the same thing as other types of values spreading through a community. The house of a healthy person isn’t any different than that of an unhealthy one.

It could be simply that the door to door solar panel salesperson was covering that 1 km area.
comments.analysis: Offers a nuanced social explanation related to aesthetics, though it questions the original conclusion's generality.

comments.author: youknownothing
comments.text:
> Early adopters of solar panels tend to be people who are interested in innovative technology, who find an installer they trust, and who think having solar panels will benefit them.

Also, conspicuous conservation, people like to signal how green they are: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S027249442...
comments.analysis: Adds a social signaling dimension to adoption behavior, supported by academic research.

comments.author: kalinkochnev
comments.text:
It's cool to see CT mentioned on HN. I haven't seen any mentions until today.

My family has installed solar on every house we've lived while there. There is a strong economic incentive. CT has some of the most expensive avg. electricity cost in the country (a few cents cheaper than California for comparison). And Eversource, the utility company, is universally hated. Most people want to switch from their terrible service and are looking for any reason to.
comments.analysis: Provides relevant economic context that supports the article's premise, though it is anecdotal.

comments.author: toss1
comments.text:
Assuming this is as reliable effect as implied in the article, a cost-effective method to jump start solar install rates would be to map out roughly 1km wide zones and provide a high and declining subsidies for the first/early people to get solar installed. E.g., go to map and find your house and zone, if five haven't signed up in your zone, it's open, the first to put deposit on install contract gets a $15k subsidy when install complete, $12k for second, $9k for 3rd, $6k 4th, $3k 5th. Adjust zone sizes and/or subsidy amounts for subsidy budget, population density, etc.

Probably a lot cheaper than a $3k subsidy for everyone, as this is only 15 $3k subsidies in each zone. Also probably a lot better to do this with everyone gets a $2.5k subsidy and the first five get the higher incentives.
comments.analysis: Proposes a practical policy based on the discussed effect, though it is speculative and untested.

comments.author: pavel_lishin
comments.analysis: Challenges a common assumption about social clustering, emphasizing direct interpersonal influence.

comments.author: pjc50
comments.text:
I wonder how effective the UK "green flash" plates (https://www.gov.uk/government/news/road-to-zero-in-sight-as-...) have been at EV marketing. I'm making a habit of looking out for them since I'm considering getting an EV myself.

Nobody on our street has one. I wonder if I get one it will spark a trend.
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about social proof in a different context, though speculative without data.

comments.author: skyberrys
comments.text: A short and sweet message in this little learning of the day post. You tend to learn from your geographic neighbors. It makes sense! Can we see the same thing for ebikes, and low water lawns?
comments.analysis: Briefly extends the discussion to other behaviors, highlighting the generalizability of the effect.

comments.author: worik
comments.text:
This reminds me to get solar.

Proximity is not just geographical.
comments.analysis: Personal comment with little analytical value, though it acknowledges broader definitions of proximity.

comments.author: grantpitt
comments.text: Somewhat analogously the best predictor of if I read a book on a given day is if I read the day before, I'd guess.
comments.analysis: Makes an analogy to habitual behavior, which is interesting but less directly relevant to the article's focus.

comments.author: SoftTalker
comments.analysis: Concise summary linking the discussion to a well-known social phenomenon, adding cultural context.
